coredata.dc:title: Novel biomass carbon dots/chitosan hydrogel-modified TiO2: An effective reduction of Cr(VI) in various wastewater under sunlight

coredata.dc:description: Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) poses a serious threat to the environment and human health owing to its inherent toxicity. Photocatalysis technology has garnered widespread attention owing to its efficiency and environmental friendliness. However, existing photocatalysts are constrained by light conditions, low reduction rates, and lack of studies in real wastewater. In this study, biomass carbon dots (P-CDs) were synthesized from peanut shell powder, then combined with chitosan (CS) and TiO2 to create a novel photocatalyst (9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS). The photocatalyst exhibited a smaller band gap and a broader light absorption range than TiO2, while the porous structure of the hydrogel increased the contact area between P-CDs/TiO2@CS and Cr(VI), thereby enhancing its photocatalytic performance. Our study demonstrates that under simulated sunlight using a xenon lamp, 0.2 g of 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS reduced 50 mg/L Cr(VI) at a rate of 99.64 % in 30 min (pH 7). The photocatalyst also exhibited excellent reduction capabilities for Cr(VI) across a pH range (pH 2–9), at different concentrations (10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 mg/L), and in various real wastewater samples (influent, effluent, and electroplating wastewater). After five cycles, the reduction rate was still maintained at 90.38 %. Experiments conducted under natural sunlight and ion interference further confirmed its outstanding photocatalytic performance and stability in real-world applications. Free radical trapping experiments indicated that electrons (e-) and holes (h+) were the primary active species. Additionally, antibacterial experiments showed that the reactive oxygen species generated by the photocatalyst under light irradiation effectively inhibited the proliferation ofEscherichia coli. Thus, P-CDs/TiO2@CS holds significant potential for practical application in environmental wastewater treatment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) poses a serious threat to the environment and human health owing to its inherent toxicity. Photocatalysis technology has garnered widespread attention owing to its efficiency and environmental friendliness. However, existing photocatalysts are constrained by light conditions, low reduction rates, and lack of studies in real wastewater. In this study, biomass carbon dots (P-CDs) were synthesized from peanut shell powder, then combined with chitosan (CS) and TiO2 to create a novel photocatalyst (9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS). The photocatalyst exhibited a smaller band gap and a broader light absorption range than TiO2, while the porous structure of the hydrogel increased the contact area between P-CDs/TiO2@CS and Cr(VI), thereby enhancing its photocatalytic performance. Our study demonstrates that under simulated sunlight using a xenon lamp, 0.2 g of 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS reduced 50 mg/L Cr(VI) at a rate of 99.64 % in 30 min (pH 7). The photocatalyst also exhibited excellent reduction capabilities for Cr(VI) across a pH range (pH 2–9), at different concentrations (10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 mg/L), and in various real wastewater samples (influent, effluent, and electroplating wastewater). After five cycles, the reduction rate was still maintained at 90.38 %. Experiments conducted under natural sunlight and ion interference further confirmed its outstanding photocatalytic performance and stability in real-world applications. Free radical trapping experiments indicated that electrons (e-) and holes (h+) were the primary active species. Additionally, antibacterial experiments showed that the reactive oxygen species generated by the photocatalyst under light irradiation effectively inhibited the proliferation ofEscherichia coli. Thus, P-CDs/TiO2@CS holds significant potential for practical application in environmental wastewater treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) is a common toxic heavy metal found in industrial wastewater, posing significant risks to environmental ecosystems and human health [1]. With the accelerated pace of global industrialization, the issue of water pollution has become increasingly severe [2]. In particular, Cr(VI) is highly toxic [3], carcinogenic [4], and persistent [5] in the environment. Organizations such as the World Health Organization (WHO) [6] and the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [7] have designated it as a priority pollutant for stringent regulation. Consequently, there is an urgent need to develop green and efficient water treatment technologies to remove Cr(VI) from aquatic systems. To date, various methods have been investigated for the removal of Cr(VI), including adsorption [8], biological methods [9], membrane filtration [10], precipitation [11], ion exchange [12], electrocatalysis [13], chemical reduction [14], and photocatalysis [15]. Among these, photocatalysis has emerged as a particularly promising approach owing to its high efficiency in water treatment.

The simplicity of operation, low energy consumption, absence of secondary pollution, and high efficiency make photocatalysis an attractive option for environmental remediation [16]. In photocatalysis, semiconductor materials such as TiO2 are activated by light. These materials possess excellent chemical stability and high photocatalytic activity [17], generating photogenerated electron-hole pairs that participate in a series of redox reactions, converting harmful pollutants into harmless or less toxic products. These substances produce photogenerated electrons (e-) and holes (h+) when exposed to light, which take part in a number of redox processes, ultimately converting harmful pollutants into harmless or less toxic products [18]. However, current photocatalysis methods for Cr(VI) reduction are mostly conducted under laboratory light sources and low Cr(VI) concentration conditions, and their practical application is often hindered by low photocatalysis rates and poor light stability. Furthermore, there is a lack of research using real wastewater samples. The photocatalytic activity of TiO2 is primarily restricted to the ultraviolet (UV) region, which significantly limits its effectiveness in real-world applications [19]. Therefore, the development of TiO2-based photocatalysts with a broader light response range remains a critical area of research.

In recent years, doping and composite strategies have been proposed to enhance the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 [20]. Carbon dots (CDs), known for their excellent light absorption properties and efficient e- transport capabilities, can effectively extend the light response range of photocatalysts [21]. Biomass-derived CDs, particularly those synthesized from peanut shell, offer numerous advantages, including widespread availability, low cost, and environmental friendliness. Meanwhile, chitosan (CS), with its natural antibacterial properties and superior structural stability, can improve the overall performance of photocatalysts [22]. Additionally, hydrogels are porous materials that offer a high specific surface area and a multitude of active sites, both of which are advantageous for augmenting the photocatalysts' ability to absorb light and catalytic activity [23]. CDs and CS are two functional materials that have gained considerable attention for their efficiency in modifying photocatalysts [24]. By combining these materials, it is possible to develop photocatalysts with enhanced catalytic efficiency and stability, making them highly promising for practical environmental applications.

In this study, we developed a novel photocatalyst, P-CDs/TiO2@CS, by integrating peanut shell-derived CDs (P-CDs) and CS into TiO2, and systematically evaluated its performance in Cr(VI) reduction, real wastewater treatment, and its antibacterial activity (Scheme 1 ). This composite strategy aims to reduce the band gap of TiO2, broaden its light absorption spectrum, and increase the contact area between the photocatalyst and pollutants, thereby enhancing its photocatalytic efficiency and practical applicability. The Cr(VI) reduction performance of the photocatalyst was investigated under various conditions, including different CD contents, material ratios, photocatalyst dosages, pH levels, and initial Cr(VI) concentrations. The practicability and stability of P-CDs/TiO2@CS as well as its potential for Cr(VI) reduction were further validated through natural sunlight exposure, ion interference, real wastewater trials, and antibacterial experiments. Additionally, material characterization and free radical trapping experiments were conducted to explore the photoreduction mechanisms. Our findings provide insights into the role of biomass-derived CDs and CS in enhancing TiO2 photocatalysis for Cr(VI) reduction, offering a new method for the efficient reduction of real Cr(VI) polluted water.
All the material details are in the supplementary materials (Text S1).

In this study, P-CDs were successfully synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. Initially, 0.5 g of ground peanut shell powder was mixed with 100 μL of EDA and 30 mL of deionized water, followed by ultrasonication to ensure uniform dispersion. After that, the mixture was poured into a 50 mL autoclave lined with Teflon and heated to 180 ℃ for 6 h. The autoclave was allowed to cool to ambient temperature following the reaction. Following that, the reaction result was centrifuged for 10 min at 4000 rpm to get rid of the big particles. After collecting the supernatant, it was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, and further purified through dialysis using a 500 Da molecular weight cutoff membrane in deionized water for 24 h. Finally, the solid P-CDs powder was obtained via freeze-drying.

The TiO2 powder and P-CDs powder were dissolved in ultrapure water to prepare P-CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites with three different P-CDs concentrations (1–3 %). To guarantee that the reactants were well mixed, the resultant mixture was aggressively agitated for 1 h. After that, the combined solution was put in a 50 mL autoclave lined with Teflon and heated for 6 h at 150 ℃. Following the reaction, the product was centrifuged to separate it and washed twice in ethanol and deionized water. Finally, the P-CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites were collected via freeze-drying for 48 h.

CS (1 g) was dissolved in 50 mL of a 2 % (v/v) acetic acid solution [23]. The solution was placed on a magnetic stirrer and stirred vigorously until it became clear, after which the P-CDs/TiO2 powder was added. The mixture was first treated with ultrasound for 30 min, followed by vigorous stirring for an additional 5 h to prepare the P-CDs/TiO2@CS composites with three different P-CDs/TiO2 ratios (9:1, 8:2, and 7:3). The resulting mixture was then freeze-dried, and the solid P-CDs/TiO2@CS composites were collected.

All wastewater samples were collected using ropes and buckets made of HDPE material and then transferred into 500 mL HDPE containers, which were sealed with lids, leaving no headspace. The sealed samples were transported to the laboratory for analysis, maintaining cold storage during transit. According to the APHA 1060 standards for sample collection and preservation [25], the samples were stored at 4 ℃ in a refrigerator for subsequent analysis. Influent and effluent samples were collected from a wastewater treatment plant (Urumqi Hexi Water Supply Co., Ltd.); further details are provided in Table S1. These samples were all obtained from the same facility. Additionally, wastewater samples were collected from an electroplating plant, and parameters such as the COD, TOC, TDS, and conductivity were measured at room temperature using a multi-parameter portable instrument (SANTIHONGKE ST-B100). Detailed data are presented in Table S2. Initial Cr(VI) concentrations were assessed for each kind of wastewater (Table S3). Additionally, ion chromatography and GCMS were used for evaluating for additional ions (Table S4) and organic materials (Table S5) found in wastewater, respectively.

In this study, a xenon lamp (PLS-SCE300D, Beijing, China) was used as the light source for the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI), simulating sunlight with an intensity of 300 W. The lamp was positioned at a constant distance of approximately 15 cm from the surface of the solution. A photocatalyst dosage of 0.2 g of P-CDs/TiO2@CS was added to each 100 mL of the 50 mg/L Cr(VI) solution. To achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium, the mixture was agitated for 10 min in the dark before photocatalytic testing. After that, the xenon lamp was turned on, and samples were taken for analysis from the Cr(VI) solution at predefined intervals. Filtration through a 0.22 μm membrane was used to remove the photocatalyst, and spectrophotometry and the diphenylcarbazide (DPC) colorimetric technique were used to calculate the residual Cr(VI) content in the solution (Text S2). Additionally, the effects of various factors on the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI), including the P-CDs content, ratio of P-CDs/TiO2 to CS, amount of photocatalyst used, different initial Cr(VI) concentrations, and pH levels, were investigated.

To further evaluate the application potential of P-CDs/TiO2@CS, five consecutive photocatalytic cycles were conducted test its stability. Furthermore, photocatalytic experiments under natural sunlight and ion interference tests were performed. Outdoor experiments were conducted in April 2024. We obtained the monthly average daily solar radiation data from August 2023 to August 2024 in Urumqi through the Xinjiang Meteorological Information Center, as shown in Fig. S1. To ensure the reliability and reproducibility of the results, the photocatalytic experiments were conducted in triplicate. To reduce random errors and guarantee data reliability, every experiment was carried out in the same way.

The collected wastewater samples were filtered through cellulose membranes (0.45 μm) pre-rinsed with ultrapure water. Thereafter, 10 mg/L of Cr(VI) was added to the filtered wastewater samples for the subsequent photocatalytic experiments. The effect of different dilution ratios on the photocatalytic performance of the influent and effluent samples was studied. During the experiments, 0.2 g of P-CDs/TiO2@CS per 100 mL of solution was added, and the photocatalytic tests were conducted under laboratory conditions on undiluted samples, 5-fold diluted samples, and 10-fold diluted samples. Furthermore, five consecutive photocatalytic cycles were performed on the influent and effluent samples to assess the stability of P-CDs/TiO2@CS in real wastewater. To further evaluate the catalytic performance of P-CDs/TiO2@CS in real-world conditions, photocatalytic experiments were also conducted under sunlight on the influent, effluent, and electroplating wastewater samples, each spiked with 10 mg/L Cr(VI).

L of deionized water was used to dissolve 10 g of yeast peptone, yeast extract (5 g) and NaCl (5 g) to create the bacterial culture medium. The pH was raised to about 7.5 with a NaOH solution (1.0 mol/L). After that, the medium was sterilized for 30 min at 121 °C to provide a sterile culture medium. A 250 mL portion of the sterile medium was placed in an incubator shaker and incubated at 37 ℃ and 120 rpm for 24 h to activate and prepare the bacterial stock solution. Subsequently, 100 mL of the sterile medium and 200 mL of the bacterial stock solution were combined, and the mixture was incubated under the same conditions until the UV absorbance at 600 nm reached or exceeded 0.5 [26].

Take the mixed bacterial solution for photocatalytic experiment (Text S3). Next, 0.2 g of the 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS photocatalyst was added, and the mixture was kept in the dark for 30 min, during which the environment was maintained at room temperature using a circulating water-cooling system. Photocatalytic antibacterial experiments were then conducted under xenon lamp irradiation (λex > 365 nm). At 30 min intervals, 0.5 mL of the bacterial suspension was sampled and plated, with samples collected three times over a total experiment duration of 90 min. The plates were incubated upside down at 37 ℃ for 24 h to observe bacterial growth.

Given that this study involves the treatment of real wastewater in practical applications, the growth trend ofEscherichia coliwas selected as the criterion to evaluate the photocatalytic disinfection performance. Photocatalytic experiments were conducted on influent samples (100 mL) with and without the addition of the photocatalyst. After a 30 min dark reaction, the samples were irradiated for 90 min, with 0.1 mL samples collected every 30 min for plating. The incubator was set to alternate between 12 h of light and 12 h of darkness. The plates were incubated upside down for 48 h at 37 ℃. The photocatalytic disinfection performance was evaluated by observing the growth morphology ofEscherichia coli.

Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix (EEM) fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). The EEM spectra were recorded at excitation wavelengths ranging from 200 to 450 nm with 10 nm increments, and at emission wavelengths from 250 to 550 nm with 5 nm increments. The excitation and emission bandwidths were both set at 5 nm, and the scan speed was 2400 nm/min. The lattice of the material was observed at 5 nm using transmission electron microscopy (TEM; Talos L120C TEM, Thermo Fisher Scientific, The USA), and the microstructure of the material was observed at 20 and 50 nm. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM; Phenom XL G2, Thermo Fisher Scientific, The USA) was performed to observe the external morphologies of the diverse photocatalysts at 10 μm. The distribution of the elements of the material was acquired through energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) at a magnification of 60 μm. The elements comprising the material were determined via energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis based on the distinct X-ray wavelengths characteristic of the different elements. The morphological alterations in E. coli before and after the photocatalytic reaction were inspected via SEM at 5 μm. Conventional scanning from 10° to 90° was conducted by X-ray diffraction (XRD; XRD-6100, Shimadzu, Japan) to detect the crystal structure of the material. The fourier transform infrared spectroscopy spectra were recorded using a Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR; Nicolet iS10, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The absorbance of the samples under different light sources was detected via ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy (UH5700, Hitachi, Japan) in the range of 200–800 nm, with a resolution of 0.5 nm. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR; MS-5000, Bruker, Germany) was employed to detect the free radicals generated during photocatalysis, such as superoxide radicals (•
O
2
-
) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and hence evaluate the active speciation capacity of the photocatalysts.

Fig. 1h presents the SEM image of P-CDs/TiO2 at 10 μm, showing the agglomerated distribution of the material. After combining with CS, the P-CDs/TiO2@CS material exhibits a highly porous structure (Fig. 1i), where the well-dispersed P-CDs/TiO2 promotes the generation of a large number of photogenerated e- and enhances the e- transfer efficiency [30]. EDS further revealed the detailed chemical composition of the P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite. The elemental distribution in Fig. S3 are shown in Fig. 1j–1 m, indicating the uniform distribution of C, N, O, and Ti within the composite material. These results demonstrate that the developed synthetic method successfully produces a P-CDs/TiO2 composite and that P-CDs/TiO2 effectively combines with CS to form a hydrogel. The porous structure of the hydrogel not only improves the dispersion of P-CDs/TiO2 but also enhances the photocatalytic performance of the composite through its rich functional groups [31]. XRD analysis were shown in Fig. 2 a, the XRD patterns of the P-CDs/TiO2 and P-CDs/TiO2@CS composites closely match the pattern of pure TiO2, diaplaying that the TiO2′ phase structure don’t change despite the modifications. The distinct diffraction peaks of pure TiO2, P-CDs/TiO2 and the P-CDs/TiO2@CS composites are in good alignment with each other, appearing at 25.24°, 37.88°, 47.99°, 54.01°, 55.05°, 62.72°, 68.92°, 70.31°, and 75.02°, corresponding to reflections from the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (116), (220), and (215) crystal planes, respectively [15,32]. Notably, slight low-angle shifts in some diffraction peaks are observed in the XRD pattern of P-CDs/TiO2@CS, possibly due to the chemical or physical interactions between P-CDs/TiO2 and CS, which subtly alter the lattice parameters of TiO2.

The FT-IR spectra of all materials is presented in Fig. S4. The stretching vibration peaks observed in the range of 3300 cm−1 to 3500 cm−1 correspond to O–H/N–H bonds, indicating that these materials possess good water solubility [33,34]. The stretching vibration peak around 1633 cm−1 is associated with C–N/C = N bonds [8], while the spectrum of P-CDs shows a C = C stretching vibration peak at 1581.41 cm−1 [35]. The characteristic peak near 1410 cm−1 results from the bending vibrations of –CH2 groups [34]. Additionally, stretching vibration peaks observed at 1138.73 cm−1 and 1141.08 cm−1 correspond to C–O–C bonds, and the peak at 1041.63 cm−1 is related to C–O bonds [35]. Notably, the characteristic peaks of TiO2 composite with P-CDs are significantly enhanced; however, this enhancement diminishes after the incorporation of CS. This reduction may be attributed to the CS coating, which could decrease the surface activity or light transmittance of the P-CDs/TiO2 composite, thereby affecting the intensity of the signal.

To further verify the structure of the photocatalytic materials, XPS was employed to analyze their chemical composition and bonding configurations. The survey XPS profiles (Fig. 2b) show that the P-CDs are primarily composed of 67.92 % carbon (C), 24.64 % oxygen (O), and 7.3 % nitrogen (N). In the P-CDs/TiO2 composite, the C content decreases to 5.27 %, whereas the O content increases to 45.74 %, titanium (Ti) accounts for 47.71 %, and the N content decreases to 1.28 %. For P-CDs/TiO2@CS, the C content increases to 18.26 % and the O content further increases to 54.65 %, possibly because of the abundant –OH and other oxygen-containing functional groups in CS. The Ti content decreases to 25.23 %, whereas the N content slightly increases to 1.87 %, possibly because of the –NH2 groups in CS. The high-resolution C 1 s XPS profiles (Fig. 2c) reveal the chemical bonding characteristics of the different materials. For the P-CDs, three peaks appear at 286.22, 284.69, and 283.19 eV, corresponding to C–O/C–N, C–C/C = C, and C–O/C–N bonds, respectively [15,36]. The P-CDs/TiO2 composite exhibits peaks at 288.29, 285.65, and 284.04 eV, corresponding to C = O, C–O/C–N, and C–C bonds, respectively. In the P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite, the peaks at 287.42, 285.05, and 283.56 eV correspond to C = O, C–O/C–N, and C–C bonds, respectively. Compared to P-CDs/TiO2, the binding energies of these bonds in P-CDs/TiO2@CS are lower, possibly due to interactions within the CS polymer chains, such as electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding, which could affect the binding energies of C = O and other bonds [15]. Fig. 2d shows the high-resolution N 1 s XPS profiles. In P-CDs, the peaks at 399.95 and 398.05 eV correspond to N–C3 and C–N = C bonds, respectively [24]. For P-CDs/TiO2, the peaks at 400.65 and 398.58 eV correspond to Ti–O–N and –N = bonds, respectively [37,38], indicating the successful incorporation of P-CDs with TiO2. In the P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite, the peaks at 400.11 and 398.68 eV correspond to O–Ti–O–N and –N = bonds, respectively [39]. The binding energy of the O–Ti–O–N bond decreases, while that of the –N = bond increases, possibly due to the interactions between the –NH2 in CS and TiO2 surface oxides. These interactions may reduce the e- density of the O–Ti–O–N bond, thus lowering its binding energy, while stronger covalent or coordination bonds form between the nitrogen atoms in CS and the P-CDs/TiO2 composite, increasing the binding energy of the –N = bond. These changes suggest that the incorporation of CS plays a significant role in modulating the surface chemical environment and bonding structure of the composite material. In the high-resolution O 1 s XPS profiles (Fig. 2e), the peaks observed at 531.42 and 529.86 eV in P-CDs correspond to O–H and O lattice oxygen, respectively [39]. In TiO2, the peaks at 530.04 and 528.41 eV correspond to lattice O and Ti = O bonds [40]. In P-CDs/TiO2, the stretching vibration peaks at 530.25 and 528.67 eV are attributed to Ti–O and Ti = O bonds, respectively [38], while in P-CDs/TiO2@CS, the stretching vibration peaks at 529.89 and 528.34 eV are also attributed to Ti–O and Ti = O bonds, respectively. The reduced binding energy of the Ti–O and Ti = O bonds in the CS-composite material further confirms the occurrence of hydrogen bonding within the P-CDs/TiO2@CS hydrogel network [41]. The Ti 2p XPS profiles are shown in Fig. 2f. TiO2 exhibits four peaks at 462.98 eV (Ti3+ 2p1/2), 461.89 eV (Ti4+ 2p1/2), 457.54 eV (Ti3+ 2p3/2), and 457.14 eV (Ti4+ 2p3/2) [29,42,43]. The P-CDs/TiO2 composite shows four peaks at 463.57 eV (Ti3+ 2p1/2), 462.62 eV (Ti4+ 2p1/2), 457.67 eV (Ti3+ 2p3/2), and 457.26 eV (Ti4+ 2p3/2). The P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite exhibits peaks at 463.26 eV (Ti3+ 2p1/2), 462.48 eV (Ti4+ 2p1/2), 457.33 eV (Ti3+ 2p3/2), and 457.09 eV (Ti4+ 2p3/2). These results suggest that the introduction of P-CDs may alter the local electronic environment of the Ti atoms via electronic interactions at the TiO2 interface. P-CDs may provide additional e- to TiO2, increasing the e- density of the Ti ions and causing a slight upward shift in the Ti 2p peak positions. The –NH2 and –OH groups in CS may interact with the TiO2 surface through hydrogen bonding or electrostatic interactions, influencing the distribution and binding energies of Ti3+ and Ti4+ [28]. The introduction of P-CDs and the role of CS contribute to these complex interfacial effects, which help to better separate and transport electron-hole pairs during the photocatalytic process, thereby enhancing the efficiency of the photocatalytic reaction.

The photocatalytic performance of the various photocatalysts was tested under simulated sunlight using a 300 W xenon lamp (λex > 365 nm). Fig. 3 a shows the Cr(VI) reduction efficiency of pure TiO2 and TiO2 doped with different ratios of P-CDs. After 30 min of adsorption equilibrium, pure TiO2 achieved a Cr(VI) reduction rate of 53.2 % after 60 min of illumination. In contrast, P-CDs/TiO2 reached a maximum reduction rate of 98.71 % under the same conditions. The superior photocatalytic performance of P-CDs/TiO2 is primarily attributed to the synergistic effect between P-CDs and TiO2. P-CDs significantly boost the interfacial charge separation and light-harvesting capacities of TiO2, expanding its light activation range and significantly improving its photocatalytic performance [44]. A comparison of the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 doped with different P-CDs contents revealed that 1 % P-CDs/TiO2 achieved the highest Cr(VI) reduction. As the P-CDs content increased, the reduction rates slightly decreased to 95.95 % and 95.66 % for 2 % and 3 % P-CDs/TiO2, respectively. This decline may be due to an excessive layer of P-CDs covering the TiO2 surface, hindering effective light penetration. Additionally, an excess of P-CDs may compete with TiO2 for photon absorption, lowering the light absorption efficiency of TiO2. Excessive nanoparticle aggregation could also reduce the surface active sites, weakening the adhesion of pollutants at the interface and thus diminishing the photocatalytic activity [45]. Therefore, appropriate P-CD loading is crucial for optimizing the photocatalytic performance of TiO2. Subsequent experiments for further investigation were conducted using 1 % P-CDs/TiO2.

1 % P-CDs/TiO2 was mixed with CS in ratios of 9:1, 8:2, and 7:3 to prepare the 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS composites, and their photocatalytic performance was studied (Fig. 3b). Owing to the porous structure of CS, the composites exhibited good adsorption performance during the dark reaction phase. As the CS ratio increased, the increased porosity facilitated contact between the photocatalyst and Cr(VI) [46]. The 7:3 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite achieved the highest Cr(VI) removal rate of 48.78 % during the dark reaction phase. Under illumination, the Cr(VI) reduction rate of the 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite stabilized and reached dynamic equilibrium within 30 min. Among the composites with different ratios, the 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite exhibited the best catalytic performance, achieving a Cr(VI) reduction of 99.61 % within 30 min. In comparison, the reduction rates for the 8:2 and 7:3 composites were 98.29 % and 97.18 %, respectively. This phenomenon may be due to the increase in the CS ratio leading to more porous structures. However, at the same time, the proportion of 1 % P-CDs/TiO2 decreased, resulting in diminished photocatalytic performance. Fig. 3c shows the photocatalytic reduction kinetics curves of these photocatalysts, derived from Eq. (1).
(1) − ln(C/C0) = kt

where C and C 0 represent the Cr(VI) concentrations at time
t
and the initial time 0, respectively, and k (min−1) denotes the rate constant of the kinetic reaction. All photocatalysts' kinetic curves showed linear relationships, according to data analysis, and all fitted P values were less than 0.05, suggesting that the pseudo-first-order kinetic model and the experimental data agreed well (Table S6). The 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite exhibited significantly superior photocatalytic performance, with a reaction rate constant of 0.16908 min−1, substantially higher than that of 1 % P-CDs/TiO2 (0.06899 min−1). Additionally, the performance of the 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite was slightly better than that of the 8:2 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS (0.12067 min−1) and 7:3 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS (0.09611 min−1) composites. Given the optimal photocatalytic activity of the 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite, further experiments were conducted to explore its potential advantages in photocatalytic degradation and provide more effective solutions for practical applications.

Experiments conducted in the dark aimed to further investigate the time required for the photocatalyst to reach adsorption equilibrium. As shown in Fig. S5, 1 % P-CDs/TiO2 reaches adsorption equilibrium within 30 min, while the 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite significantly shortens the equilibrium time to 10 min. This indicates that although the Cr(VI) removal rates of the two photocatalysts at adsorption equilibrium were similar (32.02 % and 33.46 %, respectively), the porous structure of the hydrogel markedly accelerated the contact between the photocatalyst and pollutants. These results further confirm that while the porous structure of the hydrogel has a limited impact on the extent of pollutant adsorption, it effectively shortens the contact time between the photocatalyst and pollutants, thereby enhancing the overall reaction efficiency. This characteristic suggests that the P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite has potential advantages in practical applications. In this study, the reduction Cr(VI) experiment was carried out under different light sources, including visible light, UV light, and sunlight (Fig. S6). The experimental results showed that the P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite achieved a Cr(VI) reduction rate of 60.90 % after 30 min under visible light, 57.45 % under UV light, and a significantly higher reduction rate of 99.64 % under simulated sunlight using a xenon lamp. The main causes of this discrepancy are the special qualities of the light source and the photocatalyst. Sunlight is a more complete energy source for the photocatalytic reaction since it spans a wide spectrum from visible to UV light. This broad-spectrum illumination ensures that the photocatalyst can be effectively activated across a wider range of energy levels, significantly enhancing the Cr(VI) reduction efficiency. Therefore, using sunlight as the light source can maximize the activity of the photocatalyst, resulting in superior photocatalytic performance.

To further optimize the photocatalytic conditions, the impact of varying the photocatalyst dosage (0.1–0.3 g/100 mL) on the catalytic reaction was investigated. The results indicated that increasing the amount of photocatalyst positively influenced the reaction. As shown in Fig. 3d, the Cr(VI) reduction efficiency improves when the photocatalyst concentration reaches 0.2 g/100 mL. The inset in Fig. 3d presents the pseudo-first-order kinetic model. Under a photocatalyst loading of 0.2 g/100 mL, the Cr(VI) reduction rate is 0.18072 min−1, which is 1.04 times and 1.37 times higher than 0.1 g/100 mL (0.17363 min−1) and 0.3 g/100 mL (0.13127 min−1), respectively (Table S7). This may be due to the fact that an excessive amount of photocatalyst can hinder light penetration and generate excess radicals, leading to self-quenching and thereby reducing the photocatalytic efficiency [47]. Therefore, a photocatalyst dosage of 0.2 g/100 mL was deemed optimal.

Building on the above observations, the impact of the starting Cr(VI) concentration (10–60 mg/L) on the photocatalyst's reduction activity was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 3e, the reduction time of Cr(VI) is proportional to its initial concentration when the photocatalyst dosage, reaction temperature, and solution pH are all the same. First-order reduction rate constants at various initial Cr(VI) concentrations were analyzed using linear regression (inset of Fig. 3e) provided the data summarized in Table S8, with all R 2 values exceeding 95 %, indicating that the experimental results fit the kinetic model well. At initial concentrations of 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 mg/L, the Cr(VI) reduction rates were 0.39722, 0.28165, 0.24986, 0.18657, 0.18372, and 0.12978 min−1, respectively. As the Cr(VI) concentration increased, the reduction rate gradually decreased. High initial concentrations of Cr(VI) significantly hinder the penetration of photons to the photocatalyst reaction interface, thus reducing the generation of photoactive species. Furthermore, the high concentration of Cr(VI) and its reduction products could intensify competition for the active sites on the photocatalyst [48].

The decrease of the Cr(VI) solution at various initial pH values (pH = 2–9) is shown in Fig. 3f. The pH and the existence of oxidants have an impact on the speciation and mobility of Cr in aqueous systems [49]. The major species of Cr(VI) in aqueous solutions are H2CrO4 (aq.) and HCrO4 − when the pH is below 6.5, as indicated by the Purboix diagram of Cr(VI), while they shift to CrO4 2− at pH > 6.5 [50,51]. At pH 2, the greatest reaction rate was noted. In an acidic environment, Cr(VI) might totally dissolve; however, under alkaline conditions, the reduction rates at pH 8 and 9 decreased to 89.02 % and 78.44 %, respectively. The inset in Fig. 3f presents the first-order kinetic equation under different pH levels. At pH 2, the Cr(VI) reduction rate was the highest (0.29223 min−1), which was 1.04, 1.22, 1.23, 1.62, 1.63, 5.07, and 7.58 times higher than those at pH 3 (0.28055 min−1), pH 4 (0.23956 min−1), pH 5 (0.23794 min−1), pH 6 (0.18072 min−1), pH 7 (0.17896 min−1), pH 8 (0.05768 min−1), and pH 9 (0.03857 min−1), respectively (Table S9). All R 2 values exceeded 90 %, indicating a good fit between the experimental data and the pseudo-first-order kinetic equation. The reduction rate decreased as the pH increased, possibly due to the increasing negative surface charge on the P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite as the pH increases, which intensifies the photocatalyst and CrO4 2− electrostatic repulsion [52]. This lowers the pace at which Cr(VI) is reduced by impeding its migration to the photocatalyst surface. Additionally, the stability of Cr(VI) increases under alkaline conditions [53], and the unreacted –NH2 groups on the CS molecular chains undergo deprotonation, reducing the mobility of the CS polymer chains [54]. These factors collectively contribute to the decreased catalytic performance of the P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite under alkaline conditions.

In order to evaluate the possibility for practical application of P-CDs/TiO2@CS as a photocatalyst, photocatalytic reduction studies were carried out in natural light source. The experimental conditions were 20 ℃, 21 % humidity, and a UV index of 5. In the reaction system, we selected a Cr(VI) concentration of 10 mg/L and added 0.2 g of the 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS photocatalyst. Under natural sunlight, the P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite reduced Cr(VI) at a rate of 97.06 % in 10 min, 98.24 % in 15 min, and almost completely in 20 min for 10 mg/L Cr(VI) (control curve in Fig. S12). From the standpoint of practical application, the photocatalyst's stability and reusability are essential for both its affordability and environmental preservation. Five regeneration cycles were carried out to assess the viability of P-CDs/TiO2@CS. After each cycle, the photocatalyst was only cleaned with water and dried in an oven. As shown in Fig. S7, even after five cycles, the photocatalyst maintains a Cr(VI) reduction rate of 90.38 %, demonstrating the outstanding reusability of the P-CDs/TiO2@CS photocatalyst. The FT-IR and XPS results of the recycled materials are presented in Fig. S4 and Figs. S8–S11. While slight shifts in the characteristic peaks were observed, there were no significant changes in the chemical bonds or functional groups, indicating that the materials maintain excellent stability. Furthermore, the photocatalyst achieved a recovery rate of 93.95 % at the end of the degradation experiments. This high recovery rate not only reflects the material's effectiveness but also underscores its potential for repeated use in photocatalytic applications, suggesting a promising avenue for sustainable environmental remediation.

Additionally, to evaluate the stability of P-CDs/TiO2@CS in real environmental conditions, the effect of common ions on the photocatalytic reduction process was examined. Under the same sunlight exposure conditions, ion interference experiments were conducted by introducing 10 mg/L of common metal ions (Fig. S12). The results showed that these ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cl−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, NO3 −, and NO2 −) had minimal impact on the Cr(VI) reduction efficiency of P-CDs/TiO2@CS. Fig. S13 presents the pseudo-first-order kinetic equations under different ion interferences. All linear regression models had R 2 values exceeding 95 % (Table S10), demonstrating good agreement between the experimental data and the reaction kinetic model. This indicates that P-CDs/TiO2@CS possesses strong anti-interference capabilities, maintaining a high Cr(VI) reduction efficiency in the presence of both anions and cations. In summary, P-CDs/TiO2@CS exhibits excellent Cr(VI) reduction performance and good stability under real-world conditions, effectively mitigating Cr(VI) pollution in actual environmental scenarios. This highlights its potential for practical applications, especially in the environmental remediation of wastewater containing Cr(VI).

Fig. 4 a shows the equilibrium adsorption capacity (Q e) of TiO2, 1 % P-CDs/TiO2, and 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS at different initial Cr(VI) concentrations. At the same initial Cr(VI) concentration, the Q e value of 1 % P-CDs/TiO2 is significantly higher than that of TiO2, possibly due to the spherical dispersion structure of P-CDs forming more adsorption sites at the TiO2 interface, thereby significantly enhancing the Q e of TiO2 [55]. More importantly, 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS exhibits even better adsorption performance, demonstrating a significant advantage in Cr(VI) removal. To accurately describe the adsorption isotherms of the different photocatalysts, we applied the Langmuir model (Eq. (2)) and the Freundlich model (Eq. (3)) [56,57].

Single-layer homogeneous adsorption is described by the Langmuir model; multilayer heterogeneous adsorption is described by the Freundlich model. Key parameters in the models include the theoretical maximum adsorption capacity (Q max), equilibrium concentration of Cr(VI) ions (
C e
), Langmuir adsorption constant (K L), Freundlich adsorption constant (K F), and heterogeneity factor (n). The models' goodness of fit is assessed using the R 2 value. The fitting results are displayed in Fig. 4b, and Table S11 provides a summary of the associated parameters. According to the Langmuir model analysis, the Q max of 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS was 21.73 mg/g, approximately 2.11 and 2.95 times higher than the Q max values of 1 % P-CDs/TiO2 (10.30 mg/g) and TiO2 (7.36 mg/g), respectively. This indicates that 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS has a significantly higher Cr(VI) adsorption capacity. Based on a comparison of the R 2 values, the Freundlich model described the Cr(VI) adsorption behavior of TiO2 and 1 % P-CDs/TiO2 better, suggesting that the Cr(VI) adsorption process for these two materials involves multilayer adsorption mechanisms. Conversely, the Langmuir model described the adsorption process of 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS more accurately, indicating that Cr(VI) predominantly adsorbs as a monolayer on the surface of the photocatalyst [55]. These results highlight the superior performance of 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS in Cr(VI) removal and suggest that this material has a high single-layer Q e, demonstrating its strong potential for pollutant removal in practical applications.

Fig. 4c shows the adsorption capacity (Q t) of the different photocatalysts at various contact times. The experimental data were fitted to a pseudo-first-order kinetic model in order to understand the adsorption process (Eq. (4)) [58].
(4)

- k t
where k is the pseudo-first-order rate constant. The fitting results are listed in Table S12, with all R 2 values exceeding 97 %, indicating that the relationship between Q t and t follows the pseudo-first-order kinetic model. As shown in Fig., at the same contact time, the Q t of 1 % P-CDs/TiO2 was significantly higher than that of TiO2, due to the introduction of P-CDs providing more adsorption sites on the TiO2 surface, thereby increasing the Cr(VI) removal rate. Moreover, the Q t of 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS exceeded that of 1 % P-CDs/TiO2, and 1 % P-CDs/TiO2 reached a relatively stable adsorption state within 30 min, while 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS achieved equilibrium within just 10 min. This indicates that the incorporation of CS not only enhanced the adsorption capacity of 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS for Cr(VI) but also significantly improved its adsorption efficiency. This improvement is mainly attributed to the high adsorption performance of CS and the porous structure formed after incorporation, which increased the surface area and porosity of the photocatalyst, making the Cr(VI) adsorption process more efficient [59].

The optical properties of TiO2, 1 % P-CDs/TiO2, and 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS were studied via UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS). As can be seen in Fig. 4d, the absorption edges of 1 % P-CDs/TiO2 and9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS extend to 404.96 and 422.12 nm, respectively, while pure TiO2 only absorbs light below 391.79 nm. This indicates that the introduction of P-CDs and CS played a crucial part in broadening the light response range of the photocatalyst [60,61]. Based on the data in the Tauc plots (Fig. 4e), the optical bandgap energy (
E g
) of these materials was estimated using Eqs. (5) and (6) [62].

where
a
is the absorption coefficient, A is a constant related to the transition probability,

is the speed of light (3.0 × 108 m·s−1), h is Planck's constant (6.626 × 10−34 J·Hz−1), and
v
is the frequency of the radiation. The results showed that the
E g
of pure TiO2 was 3.23 eV, while those of 1 % P-CDs/TiO2 and 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS were 3.15 and 3.03 eV, respectively. The smallest bandgap of 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS suggests that it can absorb light of longer wavelengths, thereby enhancing its photoresponse capability. Fig. 4f shows the valence-band (VB) spectra of the three materials obtained via XPS. The E VB energies of TiO2, 1 % P-CDs/TiO2, and 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS were 2.07, 2.35, and 2.81 eV, respectively. Based on the Mulliken electronegativity theory and Eq. (7), the conduction band (CB) energy of the photocatalyst can be calculated [33].

The results showed that the E CB energies of TiO2, 1 % P-CDs/TiO2, and 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS were − 1.16, −0.8, and − 0.22 eV, respectively. This indicates that 1 % P-CDs/TiO2 and 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS have narrower bandgaps compared to pure TiO2. Overall, 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS not only has the narrowest bandgap width but also exhibits the broadest light absorption range. The narrowing of the bandgap makes the material more responsive to light, thereby enhancing the photoelectric response and photocatalytic performance of the photocatalyst [54].

The catalytic activity of photocatalysts largely depends on the effective separation of photogenerated e- and h+ and the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as •
O
2
-
and •OH. Therefore, EPR measurements were performed to confirm the radicals generated during photocatalysis. As shown in Fig. 4g and 4 h, the intensity ratio of the characteristic quartet of 1:1:1:1 corresponds to •
O
2
-
[63], while the intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 corresponds to •OH [64]. Under illumination, the intensity of the characteristic peaks of DMPO-•
O
2
-
for 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS is significantly higher than that of 1 % P-CDs/TiO2, indicating that the former is more efficient in generating superoxide radicals. Similarly, the characteristic peaks of DMPO-•OH also show that the intensity of 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS is higher than that of 1 % P-CDs/TiO2. This phenomenon indicates that 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS can generate more •
O
2
-
and •OH radicals during the photocatalytic process, which is crucial for enhancing the photocatalytic activity [63]. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S14, the intensity of the TEMPO-h+ characteristic peak decreases after 15 min of illumination, indicating that some of the h+ participate in the photocatalytic reaction. Similarly, the signal peak intensity of TEMPO-e- also decreases, but to a greater extent (Fig. S15), possibly because more e- participate in the reaction. The degree of decrease in the signal peaks of h+ and e- for 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS is greater than that for 1 % P-CDs/TiO2, suggesting that the incorporation of CS effectively enhances the e- separation efficiency. By acting as an e- acceptor, CS increases the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in the photocatalyst by preventing their recombination, allowing more h+ and e- to participate in the photocatalytic reaction [31].

Using the findings of the EPR analysis, active species trapping experiments were conducted to clarify the contribution of the different radicals to Cr(VI) reduction. In these experiments, different scavengers, including BQ (for •
O
2
-
), IPA (for •OH), EDTA (for h+), and EDTA-2Na (for e-) were used to assess the impact of each radical on the reduction process (Fig. 4i). The results showed that IPA had the least impact on Cr(VI) reduction, proving that the main active species in Cr(VI) reduction is not •OH. However, when BQ and EDTA-2Na were added, the Cr(VI) reduction rates decreased to 69.97 % and 58.28 %, respectively. This suggests that •
O
2
-
and h+ contribute to Cr(VI) reduction to some extent, but t-hey are not the main driving factors. In comparison, EDTA had the greatest impact on Cr(VI) reduction, indicating that e- play the most critical role in Cr(VI) reduction. From the above, it can be concluded that •
O
2
-
and •OH are not the main active species promoting Cr(VI) reduction. Instead, e- and h+ are the primary driving forces for Cr(VI) reduction. These findings further support the conclusion that the high efficiency of the photocatalyst mainly relies on the generation and effective separation of e- and h+ rather than a single radical species.

In summary, as shown in Fig. S16, P-CDs/TiO2@CS exhibits a smaller bandgap compared to TiO2 and P-CDs/TiO2. Under light stimulation, P-CDs/TiO2@CS can accumulate more photogenerated e- and h+ at the CB and VB. These e- and h+ migrate to the catalyst surface for further reactions, where e- can convert O2 into •
O
2
-
, while h+ can convert H2O into •OH. These four types of radicals participate in the reduction reaction of Cr(VI), with e- playing a major role in the reduction process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic reduction experiments on real wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: The Cr(VI) reduction efficiency of the different photocatalysts at various dilution concentrations in influent samples from domestic wastewater exhibited significant differences (Fig. 5 a). For undiluted, 5-fold diluted, and 10-fold diluted samples, the Cr(VI) reduction rates for TiO2 were 51.52 %, 53.64 %, and 63.83 %, respectively, whereas the reduction rates for 1 % P-CDs/TiO2 significantly increased to 80.84 %, 86.29 %, and 88.31 %, respectively. Further, the P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite exhibited even better performance under these conditions, with reduction rates of 84.73 %, 88.45 %, and 96.91 %, for the undiluted, 5-fold diluted, and 10-fold diluted samples, respectively. This phenomenon may be due to the strong light response range and e- transfer efficiency of P-CDs/TiO2@CS. Additionally, the Cr(VI) concentration in the samples significantly affected the performance of the photocatalyst. Higher initial concentrations may saturate the photocatalyst surface, thus reducing reaction efficiency, whereas at lower dilution concentrations, the photocatalyst can more effectively interact with pollutants, exhibiting superior catalytic performance. Fig. 5b presents the results from the effluent samples from domestic wastewater. The Cr(VI) reduction rates of TiO2 within 60 min were 61.06 %, 65.89 %, and 70.01 % for the undiluted, 5-fold diluted, and 10-fold diluted samples, respectively, whereas those of 1 % P-CDs/TiO2 were 92.25 %, 93.44 %, and 95.56 %, respectively, and those of the 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite reached 94.83 %, 97.51 %, and 98.82 %, respectively. Similarly, cyclic experiments were conducted on the influent and effluent samples, and after five cycles, the reduction rates of 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS remained at 74.62 % and 85.84 %, respectively (Fig. 5c), further highlighting its excellent stability.

To further verify the potential of P-CDs/TiO2@CS in treating wastewater under real environmental conditions, we conducted photocatalytic experiments under natural sunlight. As shown in Fig. 5d, using 0.2 g of 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS, the Cr(VI) reduction rate reaches 86.29 % in the influent sample and 96.35 % in the effluent sample. Even in high-salinity electroplating wastewater, the reduction rate of 10 mg/L Cr(VI) reaches 78.36 %. This indicates that the photocatalyst not only performs well under standard laboratory conditions but is also effective in removing Cr(VI) in actual wastewater treatment scenarios. The inset in Fig. 5c shows that Cr(VI) reduction in different types of wastewater follows a first-order kinetic equation (Table S13), further validating its stable catalytic behavior and reliable kinetic characteristics, and thus highlighting its potential as a material for environmental pollution remediation.

Fig. 5e shows the three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence and contour plots of untreated influent domestic wastewater, while Fig. 5f shows those of the influent domestic wastewater after photocatalytic treatment with 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS. The concentration of soluble organic matter is generally positively associated with the intensity of the EEM fluorescence spectra [65]. The significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity after the photocatalytic treatment of the influent sample may be due to the reduced concentration of soluble organic matter. The structures of some organic compounds, such as UVC-like humic substances [66], aromatic compounds, and oxygen-rich compounds [67], were degraded. The 3D fluorescence results indicate that P-CDs/TiO2@CS can degrade organic pollutants to some extent. The significant reduction in the fluorescence intensity suggests that these pollutants were effectively oxidized and decomposed under photocatalytic conditions. The decrease in the fluorescence intensity also implies a reduction in the concentration of organic matter, accompanied by changes in their molecular structure, particularly the degradation of aromatic structures and oxygen-rich functional groups. These results further demonstrate the effectiveness of the photocatalytic process, indicating that P-CDs/TiO2@CS has significant practical application potential in removing organic pollutants and improving water quality.

Excellent antibacterial performance of the photocatalyst can enhance its practicality. The 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite prepared in this study contains CS, which possesses inherent antibacterial properties, endowing the photocatalyst with antibacterial activity [68]. Photocatalytic antibacterial experiments were conducted usingE. colias a model bacterium to evaluate the antibacterial effects of the 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite material in detail. As shown in Fig. 6 a, in the control group, after 30 min of dark reaction and 60 min of nonstop illumination, the bacterial colony density does not significantly alter, indicating that the temperature and light intensity under the experimental conditions had no significant impact on bacterial growth. When the photocatalyst is introduced, although following 30 min of dark response, the bacterial density does not significantly decrease, the antibacterial effect is significantly enhanced under illumination. Specifically, TiO2 shows almost no change in the bacterial colony density after illumination, indicating that TiO2 alone has minimal antibacterial capability. In contrast, 1 % P-CDs/TiO2 slightly improves the inhibition of bacterial growth; however, the effect is still insignificant. When the 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS photocatalyst was used, bacterial growth was slightly inhibited after the dark reaction, and bacterial proliferation was significantly suppressed after 30, 60, and 90 min of sunlight irradiation. This suggests that although CS has limited antibacterial effects in the dark, the 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite can effectively inhibit the growth ofEscherichia coliunder irradiation by generating a large amount of ROS. SEM images (Fig. 6b and 6c) clearly show that the surface ofEscherichia colicells treated with the photocatalyst exhibit significant damage, confirming that the 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite has bactericidal effects. Similarly, the antibacterial performance of the photocatalyst was tested using influent wastewater samples (Fig. 6d). In the control group without the photocatalyst, bacterial colonies continued to grow steadily at all stages of the photocatalytic experiment, indicating that the light intensity had no significant impact on bacterial growth. In the bacterial suspension containing 1 % P-CDs/TiO2, the bacterial density slightly decreased, while in the bacterial suspension treated with 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS, there was almost no colony growth on the culture medium. This result strongly demonstrates that the 9:1 1 % P-CDs/TiO2@CS photocatalyst exhibits excellent antibacterial performance under illumination, making it a promising material for environmental purification.

To highlight the performance of P-CDs/TiO2@CS, we compared it with established photocatalysts noted for their effective reduction of Cr(VI), as summarized in Table S14. While most studies report satisfactory Cr(VI) reduction rates, they typically operate in acidic conditions (pH 2 and 3) with low Cr(VI) concentrations (10 and 20 mg/L). In contrast, our photocatalyst achieved an impressive 99.64 % reduction rate at higher concentrations (50 mg/L) and neutral pH (pH = 7). Notably, when the pH was lowered to 2, the P-CDs/ TiO2@CS composite completely reduced 50 mg/L Cr(VI) within just 15 min. At this same concentration, our composite demonstrated superior reduction rates over a shorter duration compared to other materials. Additionally, most prior studies utilized visible light as the illumination source, which restricts the practical application of their photocatalysts in real-world scenarios. To better simulate sunlight, we employed a xenon lamp for this study and further assessed the photocatalytic performance of the P-CDs/TiO2@CS under natural sunlight. We also examined its photocatalytic activity in real wastewater and investigated its antibacterial properties, indicating that the P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite possesses significant potential for practical applications across various domains.

The photocatalyst proposed in this study contains TiO2, a commonly used catalyst in the market. P-CDs are derived from waste materials of peanut shell. CS is a unique biopolymer commercially produced from waste generated during shellfish processing[69]. Therefore, this photocatalyst not only demonstrates excellent economic feasibility but also promotes the resource utilization of waste materials, contributing to environmental protection.

Cr(VI) poses a significant threat to health and the environment. Existing photocatalytic methods are limited by factors such as pH, concentration, and light source. In contrast, P-CDs/TiO2@CS shows broad application potential and offers a promising solution to Cr(VI) pollution. While several challenges remain, including the influence of interfering substances in various wastewater in the reduction of Cr(VI), the efficient recovery of the photocatalyst and Cr(III).

In this study, we successfully developed a TiO2-based photocatalyst modified with P-CDs and CS, and systematically validated its exceptional performance in wastewater treatment. The P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite not only exhibited significant photocatalytic activity in the reduction of Cr(VI), but also demonstrated excellent effectiveness in degrading organic pollutants and inhibiting bacterial growth. UV–vis spectroscopy and XPS analysis revealed the synergistic effects of P-CDs and CS, which significantly enhanced the light response range and photocurrent response of the photocatalyst. EPR and three-dimensional fluorescence analyses further confirmed the high efficiency of the photocatalyst in radical generation and its ability to degrade complex organic compounds. Multiple cyclic experiments also demonstrated that the P-CDs/TiO2@CS composite exhibited good stability and reusability. These combined results highlight the potential of the P-CDs/TiO2@CS photocatalyst under natural sunlight, across varying concentrations, pH levels, and wastewater conditions, thereby offering valuable application prospects for photocatalytic water pollution treatment.
